## Patient Initial Report

**Patient Details**
- Age: 42-year-old
- Sex: Male

**Presenting Complaint**
- Polyuria
- Polydipsia
- Blurred vision
- Fatigue

**History**
- 10-year history of type 2 diabetes

**Vital Signs**
- Blood pressure: 140/90 mmHg

**Laboratory Values**
- Random blood sugar: 350 mg/dl
- HbA1c: 10.5%

**Treatment Initiated**
- Intravenous fluids
- Insulin infusion
- Electrolyte replacement

**Extra Details**
- Fundoscopic exam reveals microaneurysms and cotton wool spots.

## Prelim Diagnosis Report

### **1. Diabetic Hyperosmolar Syndrome**
**📌 Disease Description:**
- A metabolic complication of diabetes mellitus (DM) in which high blood sugars cause severe dehydration, increased osmolality, and a high risk of complications.

**🧠 Clinical Reasoning:**
- The patient's random blood sugar is 350 mg/dl.
- HbA1c is 10.5%.
- The patient is experiencing polyuria, polydipsia, blurred vision, and fatigue.

**✅ Recommendations:**
- Intravenous fluids
- Insulin infusion
- Electrolyte replacement

### **2. Hypertensive Retinopathy**
**📌 Disease Description:**
- Damage to the retina and retinal circulation secondary to hypertension.

**🧠 Clinical Reasoning:**
- The patient's blood pressure is elevated at 140/90 mmHg.
- The fundoscopic exam reveals microaneurysms and cotton wool spots.

**✅ Recommendations:**
- *No recommendations provided.*

### **3. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, poorly controlled**
**📌 Disease Description:**
- A chronic metabolic disorder characterized by hyperglycemia due to insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency.

**🧠 Clinical Reasoning:**
- The patient has a 10-year history of type 2 diabetes.
- The patient is currently experiencing symptoms of polyuria, polydipsia, blurred vision, and fatigue.
- The patient's random blood sugar is 350 mg/dl.
- HbA1c is 10.5%.

**✅ Recommendations:**
- *No recommendations provided.*## Best Practices Report

### **🩺 Diabetic Hyperosmolar Syndrome** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/Hyperosmolar_hyperglycaemic_state/](https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/Hyperosmolar_hyperglycaemic_state/)
  - **📖 Summary:** Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS) is a medical emergency characterized by serum hyperosmolality, hyperglycaemia, and dehydration without ketoacidosis, resulting from a relative lack of insulin. Management often includes addressing severe hyperglycaemia, high serum osmolality, and severe dehydration. Many children with type 2 diabetes and HHS may only require temporary insulin treatment, with diet and oral agents for ongoing management.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-gb/3000124](https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-gb/3000124)
  - **📖 Summary:** This source was not accessible.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/1999/1001/p1468.html](https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/1999/1001/p1468.html)
  - **📖 Summary:** Diabetic ketoacidosis and hyperosmolar diabetes contribute to thousands of deaths annually in the United States.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/21147-hyperosmolar-hyperglycemic-state](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/21147-hyperosmolar-hyperglycemic-state)
  - **📖 Summary:** Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS) is a serious, life-threatening complication of diabetes, mainly Type 2, characterized by very high blood sugar levels for an extended period, leading to severe dehydration and confusion. Stopping diabetes medications can result in high blood sugar and HHS. A key complication of HHS treatment is the potential for blood sugar levels to drop too quickly.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1914705-treatment](https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1914705-treatment)
  - **📖 Summary:** IV insulin, similar to DKA dosages, can help correct hyperglycemia in HHS patients, though many respond to fluids alone. Intensive diabetes management, including insulin therapy, is typically required initially for all HHS patients.

### **🩺 Hypertensive Retinopathy** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.verywellhealth.com/hypertensive-retinopathy-3422174](https://www.verywellhealth.com/hypertensive-retinopathy-3422174)
  - **📖 Summary:** Optometrists and ophthalmologists diagnose hypertensive retinopathy through eye exams, including dilation to examine internal eye structures.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/hypertensive-retinopathy-stages](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/hypertensive-retinopathy-stages)
  - **📖 Summary:** Hypertensive retinopathy is a progressive condition where high blood pressure damages blood vessels in the light-sensitive tissue, potentially leading to vision loss.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://health.usnews.com/doctors/hypertensive-retinopathy](https://health.usnews.com/doctors/hypertensive-retinopathy)
  - **📖 Summary:** This source helps locate doctors specializing in hypertensive retinopathy.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://health.usnews.com/doctors/hypertensive-retinopathy/california](https://health.usnews.com/doctors/hypertensive-retinopathy/california)
  - **📖 Summary:** This source helps locate doctors specializing in hypertensive retinopathy in California.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/25100-hypertensive-retinopathy](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/25100-hypertensive-retinopathy)
  - **📖 Summary:** Hypertensive retinopathy can be the first indicator of high blood pressure's impact. It increases the risk of cardiovascular diseases like coronary artery disease and stroke.

### **🩺 Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, poorly controlled** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6186054/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6186054/)
  - **📖 Summary:** A complex intervention (DECIDE) was developed to support primary care for patients with poorly controlled T2DM, targeting clinical inertia by supporting therapeutic action and GP-led medication intensification.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5602930/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5602930/)
  - **📖 Summary:** Poorly controlled T2DM involves failure to meet recommended targets for key risk factors like glycaemic control, blood pressure, and lipids.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.verywellhealth.com/managing-and-preventing-complications-of-diabetes-5114759](https://www.verywellhealth.com/managing-and-preventing-complications-of-diabetes-5114759)
  - **📖 Summary:** The American Diabetes Association recommends annual eye exams for all newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes patients. Poorly controlled diabetes increases the risk of heart disease and heart failure, especially in overweight individuals with a family history of heart disease, high blood pressure, and elevated cholesterol.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19817515/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19817515/)
  - **📖 Summary:** Managing type 2 diabetes effectively often requires a combination of programs to help patients succeed in a cost-effective manner.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31239180/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31239180/)
  - **📖 Summary:** Patient-centered consultations can improve glycemic control and self-management skills in patients with poorly regulated type 2 diabetes compared to usual care.
